newsSinceJun6: FY25/26 preliminary results published June 9, 2026: revenue -4.6% to GBP 423.2m, adjusted operating profit -7.3% to GBP 73.7m (17.4% margin) including a GBP 5.5m FX headwind. Order intake +6.4% to GBP 450.4m, with Advanced Technologies orders +28% OCC driven by compound semiconductor demand; FY27 revenue largely covered by the order book and the share buyback was extended to GBP 100m. Shares fell -9.3% on the print - largely sell-the-news after a +36% YTD run, since the headline numbers had been pre-announced in line in the April 14 trading update; the AI-relevant semiconductor-tooling thesis actually strengthened.

sources.label: Investegate - Oxford Instruments Preliminary Results RNS (9 June 2026)
Oxford Instruments full-year trading update (14 April 2026)

verdicts.rankRationale: On a latent-AI-leverage list, today's -9.3% sell-the-news de-rate hit a name whose AI-adjacent evidence just got stronger: Advanced Technologies orders +28% on compound semiconductor demand with FY27 revenue largely covered. The drop resets the post-run crowding discount that capped it yesterday, improving residual asymmetry with the thesis intact.
verdicts.bull: Compound-semi tooling orders +28% with a multi-year April order covering most of FY27 revenue confirms genuine, under-appreciated AI hardware-supply leverage; the GBP 100m buyback and a 9% cheaper entry add asymmetry.
verdicts.neutral: Group revenue -4.6% and AOP -7.3% were pre-announced in April, so the print contained little incremental news either way; YTD is still +36%, so this is a partial, not full, reset.
verdicts.bear: Imaging & Analysis stays tariff-exposed and soft, the GBP 5.5m FX drag persists into FY27, and the semiconductor order surge could prove a cyclical spike rather than structural AI pull-through.
verdicts.primaryCatalyst: Conversion of the +28% Advanced Technologies order book into FY27 revenue and further large multi-year compound-semiconductor wins.
verdicts.primaryRisk: FX and tariff headwinds plus continued Imaging & Analysis weakness compress margins and overwhelm the semiconductor order momentum.
